We're pair-programming on my project in Visual Studio Code. I'm at the keyboard — I paste files for you, run the commands you suggest, and report back what happens. You're the engineer driving the work.

So our back-and-forth stays smooth, please use these three plain-text conventions when relevant:

1) To change a file, write an edit block:

EDIT: relative/path/to/file.ext
<<<<<<< SEARCH
(the exact existing lines to replace)
=======
(the new lines)
>>>>>>> REPLACE

   The SEARCH text must match the file exactly. Use several EDIT blocks for several changes. To create a brand-new file, use FILE_WRITE instead:

FILE_WRITE: relative/path/to/new-file.ext
```
(full file contents)
```

2) To see a file you need but I haven't pasted yet, ask for it on its own line and stop there — I'll paste it back next message:

SHOW: relative/path/to/file.ext

3) When a terminal step would help (run tests, install a dep, git, build, check a URL with curl), put the single next command in a fenced bash block. I'll run it on my machine and paste the real output back:

```bash
npm test
```

Working agreement:
- For anything beyond a quick one-off, START by sending a plan: a line "PLAN:" followed by a numbered checklist of concrete, ordered steps (e.g. data model, server scripts, client UI, wiring, polish). Keep steps small and buildable.
- Then build it one step at a time. Each turn, do the next unchecked step (its EDIT/SHOW/bash), and when that step is finished write "TASK_DONE: N" for its number. I'll show you the running checklist with what's done each turn so we never lose the thread — even across a long build.
- One step at a time. Don't list alternative commands as several bash blocks — pick the single next command, see the result, then decide.
- Read before you edit: if you're unsure what's in a file, SHOW it first.
- Self-verify every file you write: after a FILE_WRITE or EDIT, immediately do a SHOW: on that file and scan it for obvious mistakes (syntax errors, missing returns, wrong structure) before marking the step done. If you spot a bug, fix it in the same turn — never leave a known-broken file and move on.
- When every checklist item is done, write a short line starting with DONE: summarizing what was built.
- You don't need permission for each step — just propose the edit or command and I'll carry it out.

I've included the project's file tree and the most relevant file contents below. Start from there; SHOW: anything else you need.

I've also got some project tools wired up that I can run for you and paste the result back — same deal, you ask and I run it:

From Roblox_Studio:
- Roblox_Studio.search_game_tree — Explore the Roblox game hierarchy tree with flat JSON output.
    • head_limit (number): Maximum number of results to return. Default: 200. Prevents overwhelming output for large trees.
    • instance_type (string): Filter by ClassName using IsA() check. Examples: 'BasePart', 'BaseScript', 'GuiObject', 'Model', 'Folder'. Case sensitive.
    • keywords (string): Filter by instance name keywords (case-insensitive). Separate multiple keywords with commas or spaces. Instance name must contain at least one keyword. Examples: 'player', 'red, blue', 'button door'
    • max_depth (number): Maximum absolute depth to traverse from start point. Default: 3, Absolute max: 10. Limits how deep we explore in the hierarchy regardless of filters. Beyond this depth, children are summarized instead of expanded.
    • path (string): Start exploration from this path. Examples: 'Workspace', 'ServerScriptService', 'Workspace.Models'. Path is case-sensitive.
- Roblox_Studio.script_search — Fast script search based on fuzzy matching against script names.
    • keywords (string) (required): The comma-separated keywords string to search for in the game's scripts. Each keyword is case-insensitive.
- Roblox_Studio.get_studio_state — Get the state of the studio, including current play state and available datamodel types.
- Roblox_Studio.screen_capture — Capture current edit-time screen, return the image data.
    • camera_position (array): The position of the camera to capture the screen from.
    • capture_id (string) (required): Capture identifier such as 'ScreenCapture_1', 'ScreenCapture_2', etc.
    • look_at_position (array): The position to look at.
- Roblox_Studio.get_console_output — Get the console output from the Studio output log.
- Roblox_Studio.script_grep — Runs a search for a string pattern over all script contents in the game.
    • query (string) (required): The string or Luau pattern to search for.
- Roblox_Studio.execute_luau — Executes Luau code in Roblox Studio.
    • code (string) (required): The Luau code to execute.
    • datamodel_type (string) (required): The target datamodel to operate on, the tool can only be performed in those datamodel types. call get_studio_state to get current available datamodel types. if the target datamodel is not available in current mode, consider using start_stop_play to switch to the desired mode and then use the tools. This is a required argument. — one of: Edit | Client | Server
- Roblox_Studio.start_stop_play — Start play the game or stop the play.
    • is_start (boolean) (required): true to start the game, false to stop the game and return to edit mode.
- Also available (ask me to describe one before calling it): http_get, character_navigation, skill, search_creator_store, subagent, multi_edit, generate_mesh, upload_image, wait_job_finished, generate_procedural_model, user_keyboard_input, user_mouse_input, generate_material, script_read, insert_from_creator_store, store_image, inspect_instance, list_roblox_studios, set_active_studio

To run one, write its name on its own line then a fenced ```json block with the arguments, like:

MCP: Roblox_Studio.http_get
```json
{ }
```

I'll run all of them and paste back every result at once.

Batching rule: READ-ONLY calls (search_game_tree, script_search, etc.) can be batched — write up to 4 MCP blocks in one turn and I'll run them all. Any call that changes state must go one at a time so you see the result before proceeding.

Roblox Studio scripts — IMPORTANT: a path like `game.ServerScriptService.Bar`, `game.ReplicatedStorage.Foo`, or `game.StarterPlayer.StarterPlayerScripts.Baz` is a Studio script, and you edit it with the SAME conventions as any file — I route them straight into Studio for you:
  • read one with `SHOW: game.ServerScriptService.Bar` — you get the live source back
  • change one with an `EDIT: game.ServerScriptService.Bar` block (`<<<<<<< SEARCH` / `=======` / `>>>>>>> REPLACE`)
  • create or fully replace one with `WRITE_FILE: game.ServerScriptService.Bar` and a fenced code block
Do NOT hand-write `script_read` / `multi_edit` MCP calls for scripts — just use SHOW:/EDIT:/WRITE_FILE on the `game.*` path and I handle the Studio plumbing. Use the MCP tools above for the things SHOW/EDIT can't do (exploring the tree, searching, running one-off Luau). If a SHOW comes back "does not exist in Studio yet", create it with WRITE_FILE. If an EDIT comes back "SEARCH didn't match", SHOW: the script and fix your SEARCH text against the live source.

Use the EXACT parameter names and allowed values listed under each tool above. Do not invent or rename parameters (e.g. if the write tool's argument is `file_path`, never switch to `target_file`). For any parameter shown as "one of: …", you must pass one of those literal values — never guess a value that isn't in that list.

If an MCP call comes back `ERROR` complaining about a missing/invalid/unknown argument: re-read this tool's parameter list, fix the specific argument it named, and resend ONCE with corrected args. Do not retry the identical call, do not guess enum values one-by-one across turns, and do not fall back to the disk conventions — that just loops. If the parameter list above genuinely doesn't show what a required arg expects, say so plainly and ask, rather than brute-forcing guesses.

Reference playbooks I have on hand — if one fits, write `SKILL: name` on its own line and I'll paste the full guide:
- roblox-game-systems — Roblox server systems — retention loops, DataStore persistence, monetization compliance, receipts, referrals
- roblox-rng-ui — Build polished, high-fidelity Roblox RNG/gacha UIs in Luau with real "game juice"

Got it — this is a big merge. Before I touch anything, let me lay out a concrete plan, then we'll walk through it one step at a time. I'm going to use **Onekey-main** as the chassis (it's already a Wails Go + Vue app that does manifest downloading + SteamTools patching — the closest thing to your goal), and pull the other repos in as features/data sources rather than trying to fuse 4 different languages into one binary.

PLAN:
1. **Audit the chassis.** Read Onekey's `README.md`, `app.go`, `api.go`, `main.go`, and `internal/manifest/handler.go` so I understand exactly how it currently downloads/parses manifests and writes Lua. This sets the integration surface.
2. **Audit the donor projects.** Skim the four other "manifest" tools so I know what each adds:
   - `Manifest2Lua-main/main.py` — Python `.manifest → .lua` converter (we'll reimplement in Go inside Onekey).
   - `ManifestDownload-main/ManifestDownload/Program.cs` + `DepotKeyStore.cs` — C# depot-key fetcher (logic we'll port into a Go `depotkeys` package).
   - `ManifestHub-main/{appaccesstokens.json,depotkeys.json}` — bundled key/token data → ship as embedded JSON via `go:embed`.
   - `GameList-main/games.json` — game catalog for the picker UI.
   - `ManifestHub2-50300/*` — golden sample (game 50300) used as an integration test fixture.
3. **Repo layout.** Reorganize into one root project (`SteamToolbox/` or similar) with:
   - `cmd/steamtoolbox/` (main entry)
   - `internal/manifest/` (download + parse, from Onekey)
   - `internal/lua/` (Manifest2Lua port)
   - `internal/depotkeys/` (ManifestDownload port + embedded ManifestHub data)
   - `internal/gamelist/` (games.json loader)
   - `internal/steamtools/` (existing Onekey SteamTools integration)
   - `frontend/` (Onekey's Vue UI, extended)
   - `assets/data/` (embedded JSON: depotkeys, appaccesstokens, games)
   - `testdata/50300/` (ManifestHub2 sample)
4. **Data model.** Define shared Go types: `AppInfo`, `DepotInfo`, `ManifestRef`, `DepotKey`, `GameEntry`, `LuaOutput`. One place, used by every package.
5. **Embed bundled data.** Add `go:embed` for `depotkeys.json`, `appaccesstokens.json`, `games.json`; expose getters: `depotkeys.Get(depotID)`, `gamelist.Search(query)`, etc.
6. **Port Manifest2Lua → `internal/lua`.** Read the Python; replicate `.manifest` parsing + Lua emission in Go. Unit test against `ManifestHub2-50300/50300.lua` as the expected output.
7. **Port ManifestDownload → `internal/depotkeys`.** Replicate the depot-key fetch + cache flow in Go. Wire it into the manifest handler so unknown depots can be resolved at runtime, with the embedded ManifestHub set as the fallback.
8. **Wire backend API.** Extend `app.go` / `api.go` with Wails-bound methods: `SearchGames`, `ResolveApp(appID)`, `DownloadManifest(appID)`, `ExportLua(appID)`, `InstallToSteamTools(appID)`.
9. **Extend the Vue UI.** Add a "Manifest Downloader" view that mimics SteamTools' flow: search → pick game → resolve depots → download → preview Lua → "Add to Steam" via existing SteamTools integration. Reuse `GamesView.vue` / `DlcDetailView.vue` as starting points.
10. **End-to-end smoke test.** Use game 50300 (we have the full fixture). Run the pipeline and `diff` the generated `.lua` against `ManifestHub2-50300/50300.lua`.
11. **Build + packaging.** One `wails build` command produces the single binary. Update root `README.md` documenting the merged project and crediting each upstream repo + their licenses.
12. **Polish.** Settings page hookup for cache/output paths, i18n strings (en/zh — Onekey already has both), error toasts, and a "Sources" panel listing embedded data versions.

A couple of upfront calls I'm making — push back if you disagree:
- **Drop SteamTools-develop from the merge as code.** It's a 1000+ file .NET/Avalonia desktop app — fusing it into a Go/Wails project isn't a merge, it's a rewrite. We'll *mimic* its manifest-downloader flow (that's what you actually asked for), and keep the SteamTools repo on disk only as a reference/UX inspiration.
- **Reimplement Manifest2Lua and ManifestDownload in Go** rather than shelling out to Python + .NET. Single binary, no runtime deps, cross-platform via Wails.

`Manifest2Lua-main/main.py` — Python `.manifest → .lua` converter (we'll reimplement in Go inside Onekey).
`ManifestDownload-main/ManifestDownload/Program.cs` + `DepotKeyStore.cs` — C# depot-key fetcher (logic we'll port into a Go `depotkeys` package).
`ManifestHub-main/{appaccesstokens.json,depotkeys.json}` — bundled key/token data → ship as embedded JSON via `go:embed`.
`GameList-main/games.json` — game catalog for the picker UI.
`ManifestHub2-50300/*` — golden sample (game 50300) used as an integration test fixture.
**Repo layout.** Reorganize into one root project (`SteamToolbox/` or similar) with:
`cmd/steamtoolbox/` (main entry)
`internal/manifest/` (download + parse, from Onekey)
`internal/lua/` (Manifest2Lua port)
`internal/depotkeys/` (ManifestDownload port + embedded ManifestHub data)
`internal/gamelist/` (games.json loader)
`internal/steamtools/` (existing Onekey SteamTools integration)
`frontend/` (Onekey's Vue UI, extended)
`assets/data/` (embedded JSON: depotkeys, appaccesstokens, games)
`testdata/50300/` (ManifestHub2 sample)
**Data model.** Define shared Go types: `AppInfo`, `DepotInfo`, `ManifestRef`, `DepotKey`, `GameEntry`, `LuaOutput`. One place, used by every package.
**Embed bundled data.** Add `go:embed` for `depotkeys.json`, `appaccesstokens.json`, `games.json`; expose getters: `depotkeys.Get(depotID)`, `gamelist.Search(query)`, etc.
**Port Manifest2Lua → `internal/lua`.** Read the Python; replicate `.manifest` parsing + Lua emission in Go. Unit test against `ManifestHub2-50300/50300.lua` as the expected output.

**Port ManifestDownload → `internal/depotkeys`.** Replicate the depot-key fetch + cache flow in Go. Wire it into the manifest handler so unknown depots can be resolved at runtime, with the embedded ManifestHub set as the fallback.
**Wire backend API.** Extend `app.go` / `api.go` with Wails-bound methods: `SearchGames`, `ResolveApp(appID)`, `DownloadManifest(appID)`, `ExportLua(appID)`, `InstallToSteamTools(appID)`.

**Extend the Vue UI.** Add a "Manifest Downloader" view that mimics SteamTools' flow: search → pick game → resolve depots → download → preview Lua → "Add to Steam" via existing SteamTools integration. Reuse `GamesView.vue` / `DlcDetailView.vue` as starting points.
**End-to-end smoke test.** Use game 50300 (we have the full fixture). Run the pipeline and `diff` the generated `.lua` against `ManifestHub2-50300/50300.lua`.
**Build + packaging.** One `wails build` command produces the single binary. Update root `README.md` documenting the merged project and crediting each upstream repo + their licenses.
**Polish.** Settings page hookup for cache/output paths, i18n strings (en/zh — Onekey already has both), error toasts, and a "Sources" panel listing embedded data versions.
